sentences.text: I was talking about the-- taking care of the necessary documentation in order to leave for the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to make several trips to Antwerp to-- I guess the American consulate was located there.
It was not a long trip for us.
We had to take my brother a couple of times.
The last time we took him, which was the most important time, where he had to-- all of us had to swear that the information we had provided in our applications was the truth.
You know, anyone who gets a passport has to do that.
The whole truth and nothing but the truth.
And my brother all of a sudden decided he didn't want to do that.
He didn't want to swear that the information provided was the truth.
And, of course, immediately the, the official in charge questioned his mental state and the veracity of the information we had provided on his behalf.
And I have to tell you that my mother had bribed a doctor to give him a, a clean bill of health.
Everyone was so hungry in Belgium, it wasn't difficult to bribe anyone.
And my mother, being my mother, you know-- nothing fazed her.
Survival was-- had become an art with her.
And knowing which way to turn was her strength.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But at that point, there was nothing she could do.
And this time they-- the consul sent my brother to their doctor, who, of course, declared him mentally unfit.
So he was denied a visa.
And this was, of course, another blow for my mother, who had just found her children and now was about to lose one of them again, although she did feel that he was in good hands.
And perhaps, although she never said that, wondered what we would do with him, had we brought him to the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But the reality was that we did leave, eventually, in March of 1946.
We got, we got passage on a cargo boat.
At that time, there were no passenger ships.
All the ships had been requisitioned for the army, for the soldiers.
So there was no-- there were none, as far as I know, that were exclusively being used for passengers.
And there were just a handful of us on the ship who were not part of the crew or-- I don't know what this ship was carrying, and I really didn't care.
My mother and I-- and we took one other child with us.
Somehow, my mother had found out that this child had no one to accompany her, and she could not leave without being accompanied.
This was a Jewish child.
I have no idea where she unearthed this child.
So-- but we-- she said that we would take care of this child, which we did.
The other passengers were all war brides.
There were maybe a dozen of them, all of them young, Belgian war brides, who were absolutely terrified of what they had [LAUGHS] let themselves in for.
And some even flirted with these, with, with, with the, with the crew, which I found absolutely incredible.

But I often wondered how they fared, coming to this country and meeting their husbands, whose language they probably couldn't speak and meeting their in-laws and, you know, the whole family and this very strange way of life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nowadays, again, you know, nothing is strange.

The, the television and the media, you know, sort of has homogenized so many societies, so many countries, that to move or to, to travel, you know, the language may pose a problem, but the way of life is not that different.
At that time, coming to the United States from Belgium was like coming from a-- I often compare it-- from a Victorian society into the late 20th century.
That's how I saw it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We finally got on this ship, which took two weeks.
And it was a very rough passage.
And, in any case, I was very nervous.
I, I didn't mind leaving the orphanage, although I did wonder-- I mean, I was very excited about coming to the United States, like everyone else in Europe.
At that time, the United States-- there was nothing like it.
You know, it was the country to be in and the place where you would find-- where your dreams would come true.
And we were very fortunate.
We knew that, that we had the opportunity to come to the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My, my father had sent me some clothes, as I mentioned earlier.
And one of the items that he had sent me was a red Chesterfield coat.
It was a coat with a black velvet collar.
I'll always remember it.
And as the ship was approaching New York, my mother told me to put on my coat, which I really resented.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, again, I was sort of being singled out.
I was being-- I don't know, you know.
I knew, of course, why I had to do it.
I hadn't seen my father in almost eight years.
And I certainly was not the same child.
And perhaps my mother was afraid that he would not recognize her, because she had changed a lot.
At least in my mind she had changed a lot.
She was-- when I saw her-- going back a little bit-- she didn't look anything like the woman that I remembered, perhaps, perhaps because she was so poorly dressed.
I didn't mention that.
And her arm had been broken in the camp.
She had been shot.
And so her arm was sort of deformed.
And it remained that way for the rest of her life.
She refused to have it fixed.
She said, I can use my arm.
My hands-- you know, she can do most things with her hand.
She didn't want to take a chance on having somebody, you know, fiddle around with it and maybe make it worse than it was.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] perhaps she thought that nobody would recognize her.
And so I, you know, with my red flag-- they couldn't help seeing me.
So eventually I, I agreed.
I put on my coat.
And here was this huge family that was meeting us at the boat-- aunts, uncles, cousins, what have you-- cousins on my mother's, on my mother's side-- her first cousins.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father was, to me, a total stranger.
I had never been close to my father.
He was a very quiet person, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would never have recognized him, although I'd seen a picture which he had sent to my mother.
Didn't look like the picture, either.
And I think it was a very difficult reunion, as you can well imagine.
I mean, what do you say-- what does a father say to a child he hasn't seen in eight years?
What does he say to a wife he hasn't seen in six years and who comes from a different planet?
Her experiences and her, you know, way of life for those six years had been so horrendously difficult that nobody could imagine what it was like.
And you know, I often thought that my experience also was so different from anybody else's.
And certainly all of a sudden being reunited, not only with a mother but with a father-- what do you do with a mother and father?
I didn't know.
I mean, I was very conditioned to a very authoritarian life, to a very rigid, regimented life.
Not that I liked it, but I was almost conditioned to, to live in that fashion and to not to really need a father and a mother.
I mean, I was grown up by now.
I was 16, but I felt like as if I was 50.
What, what did I need a mother and a father for?
I could take care of myself, if not physically-- I mean, you know, at least I didn't need them to tell me what to do or, or what not to do or when to eat and when not to eat.
I didn't think I needed them for moral support or for emotional support.
I-- you know, to me, they were--
I felt very strongly about my mother, at a certain level, because I felt terrible about her experience.
And I always felt that, if nobody else, I had to make it up to her.
So I had, you know, a very ambivalent feeling about her.
At the same time, I had a very difficult time getting along with her.
She was this very strong, very willful person who had many demands.
And I was not exactly the pliable child that she may have known when I was eight, when I was a little child.
I was now an adult, although to her I was still a child.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Bonding I don't believe ever happened.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We superficially were a family.
In reality, I don't think we were.
We had many problems.
First of all, I was terribly unhappy in the United States.
I hated it.

I came, you know, thinking that all my problems would be solved, that nothing-- you know, that there would not be any more negative experiences, that I somehow would sail along, go to school, have a career, and get married, and, you know, all the sort of stuff that you read in, in fairy tales-- that all this would happen, simply because that's the only way of life that is possible in these great United States.
I was very naive, but I didn't know any-- you know, and I didn't know anything else.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to school.
When I came to the United States, I know, you know, I had theoretically lost three years of schooling.
And, in their wisdom, the school-- Bassick High School, in Bridgeport, Connecticut-- decided to sort of-- I was the first foreigner that came to that school without knowing English.
And eventually two more kids came.
But this was a totally new experience for them.
And they decided to just send me to English classes.
So I went from one English class to another.
So I lost another year, in fact.
So, by the end of that year, I was now four years behind.
But I sort of fibbed my way through.
I had brought no records.
And when I could speak enough English, after that term when I attended nothing but English classes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told them that I should be in the 10th grade.
By now I was 16.
And so they allowed me to go into the 10th grade.
And I-- by taking extra courses and going to summer school, I finished high school in two years.
So I graduated-- and I was so proud of that-- almost with my own age group.
I was very, very concerned.
I, I didn't want to be-- again, I didn't want to be different.
I wanted to be-- this became one of my goals, when I arrived in the United States.
I didn't care anymore whether I was Jewish, whether I was Christian, whether I was black, whether I was white.
You know, I just wanted to, to be left alone and not to be singled out and to just do my own thing, without being, you know, pointed out or, or talked about.
And yet, when I came, I had braids, which I wore on top of my head.
And I had never worn makeup.
I was 16.

I had never worn stockings, so I-- and I rebelled against-- you know, my relatives immediately wanted me to get dressed and to wear lipstick, and, and-- I had humongous fights with my relatives, because I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, I wouldn't allow them to dominate me.
And yet they certainly tried.
And they thought that they had every right to do so, because they wanted to Americanize me.
I understand what they were trying to do.
I resented it.
And yet I wanted to be like everybody else, but I must have stuck out like a sore thumb.
[LAUGHS] It was-- you know, thinking back on it, it was a very ironic way of behaving.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life with my parents wasn't working out.
We were fighting all the time.
And I stayed home for about two years, and then I left.
I went to school in New York and never really lived at home anymore.
And that was-- I used to come home a lot, almost every weekend.
But at least I was not in the house.
I went to a secretarial school.
I had no idea what to do with my life.
I had no clue.
Didn't know anything about college.
And there was nobody to help you.
You know, these days you have guidance counselors.
You have social workers.
You have a host of people who are there to help you.
There was nobody in '46, '47, '48 at Bassick High School, at least, nobody that I ever discovered or nobody that ever discovered me and thought, well, this, this kid really needs some guidance.
That never happened.
So I knew I had to earn a living.
And somehow I heard about this secretarial school, so I decided that's where I was going to go.
When I finished secretarial school, by that time I knew a little bit more about college and so on, and I decided to go to college.
And I started going to night classes at City College.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And eventually, many years later, I got my bachelor's and master's in, in Toronto.
It took a-- [LAUGHS] took a long, a long while.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, you know, that basically is, you know, the outline of my story.
Yes, I, I was married in 1953 [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and moved to Scotland, which was-- my late husband was a psychologist, and he got a position at the University of Aberdeen.
And so we left.
Again, my mother in tears-- I'll never see you again, and how can you do that to me?
And, you know, that was a nice farewell to me.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, I had no problems leaving.
I, I hated Bridgeport.
I, I, you know, I cared for my family on one level.
On the other level, I could not get along with them.
I really had nothing in common with them.
That was the reality.
I had nothing in common with the other German Jews that my parents associated with almost exclusively.
I mean, some of these relatives I'm now very fond of, actually.
But at that time, I was not interested in them.
And I didn't think they were interested in me.

They were only interested in fashioning me according to their own image, you know, to wear the lipstick, to cut your hair, and, and to wear stockings and this kind of dress, and, and join this, this sorority, and, and--
Well, not, not immediately.
They were really pressuring me almost immediately.
And it was too early.
It took me a long time.
I think it probably took years.
Even though I wanted to be like everybody else, there was a limit to how far I was willing to go in order to be like everybody else.
I just wanted to do my own thing very quietly.
Nobody ever asked me.
I don't recall anybody ever asking me my story--
Well, at first I didn't know any English.
But I learned it very quickly.
I arrived in March, and that summer I took a course in Algebra.
So I already knew enough English to, to take a course in Algebra.
Now, I purposely took a math course because I knew that, with a little bit of English, I could get by.
And by the end of that summer, I took a, I took more than the full course, because I was anxious to-- I knew how many credits I had to make up.
And so I took-- you know, I had it all mapped out.
I was going to graduate at 18 and so I had-- and the school allowed me to do anything I wanted to.
They didn't know what to do with me or how to take me, you know.
I could have told them I was in the 12th grade, and I should only take 12th-grade courses, and they probably would have let me do that, too.
Three years.
Yeah, my oldest daughter was born in Aberdeen, Scotland, shortly before we left.
We went to California-- to Berkeley.
No, to Stanford.

My husband got a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was awarded a fellowship at a place called the Center for Advanced Study in the Behavioral Sciences, which is-- which, at that time, was funded by the Ford Foundation and which is located in Stanford.
It still is, in fact.
We were there a couple years ago for a family affair, and we visited the place, which is still there and which is still functioning.
Scholars are invited to spend a year there doing whatever they want to.
It was a wonderful place.
So we left Aberdeen.
And I didn't care for Aberdeen.
And I-- it's a small town.
My husband didn't, either.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we, we never really-- we never went back to Aberdeen.
We, we, we left.
And we became wanderers.
We went Berkeley one year and-- Stanford one year and Berkeley one year.
After that, we moved to Geneva to work with Piaget.
I don't know if you've heard of Jean Piaget, a very famous psychologist.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my husband was very interested in his work, and it was a privilege to go in and work with him for a year.
We could have stayed longer, but again, he was a very domineering figure.
And my husband liked doing his own thing and not other people's research projects.
So we left, and we wandered around a bit, until we ended up in, in Toronto.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We went to Toronto in '62.
And my husband died in '76, so he was not there very long, actually-- 13 years.
But they were good years.
We had two more children in, in between.
One was born in Toronto, actually.
The third one was born in Toronto, the youngest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I continued, of course, being in touch with my parents.
They came to see me.
They came to Europe once, to visit my brother-- and visited my brother.
The only time they visited my brother, which is very strange, but anyhow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think, in a sense, they abandoned him.
And again, the responsibility fell on me to write or to visit since I was in Europe far more than they were.
And I accepted it.
But looking back on it, I'm, I'm angry that they didn't really put themselves out more to see him.
I'm sure they had good reasons.
We never talked about it.
They'd asked me to do it, and I did it.
They sent him things.
They sent  him clothes, which he probably didn't need.
And they sent money when-- some of the families he stayed with demanded money, so they sent money.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But later, in the '80s, after my father died, my mother went one more time.
We took her on one of our yearly trips.
And so she only saw him-- between '46 and the rest of her life, she only saw him two more times.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

Yes, I stayed in Toronto until I married my present husband, which, which was really a very fortunate thing, not only because I was only 46 when I lost my husband-- I was still a young woman-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but because my parents lived in Bridgeport.
And so I was able to help them, you know, as they got older and, and, and their health was no longer as good.

I mean, I always liked-- even though our, our personal relationship was not of the-- you know, it was not of the best quality, you might put it that way, I always felt very keenly-- I was always very anxious that they be well taken care of.
And it was much easier to do it from here than from Toronto.
My father was a very sickly person, but my mother took care of him.
She was very strong.
But in, in the last couple of years, she herself got very sick.
And so I was very glad that I was here and was able to look after her.
Now you are devoting yourself to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to recalling and to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bringing out so much of your experiences.
Could you tell us about that?
Yeah, yeah.
Well, because my mother was a concentration-camp survivor, I never considered myself a survivor.
I considered myself somebody who'd maybe, you know, had an unusual childhood.
And in fact, I didn't think my childhood was that bad.
I thought I had learned a lot in the orphanage.

I felt that I was far better equipped to deal with life-- little did I know-- than my contemporaries in the United States, you know, who, who, you know, depended on their parents, who had to ask permission to do this and to do that.
And now I know it was all rationalization, but at that time I certainly didn't know it.
And I even considered that once I had children, I should send them away for a while to live, you know, in an institution, to learn to be away from home, not to depend on your parents.
Because parents, you know, are not necessarily dependable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I seriously thought about that.
Strange.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But of course, I, I, I never carried that out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot the question.
What, what are the activities now that you're involved in?
Oh, I see.
Holocaust work.
Yes.
So it was very gradual for me to realize that perhaps my experiences weren't that wonderful and that I, no less than my mother, deserved to be recognized as a survivor.
Why is that important?
I'm not sure.
But it was important, because at least I could come out with my story.
I never really talked about my childhood.
I didn't think I had very much to talk about.
I was raised in an institution.
So what?
I didn't, I didn't feel that I necessarily lacked anything.
How would I know what I lacked?
You know, what did I know about being brought up in a home with a mother and a father and maybe some siblings and other relatives around?
I didn't know.
I couldn't compare it.
I couldn't compare my life to somebody who had a more quote-unquote 'normal' childhood.
So I really, you know, was at a loss as to how to handle my childhood, vis-a-vis that of a what we might call a normal person.
It came-- it hit me the first time, when my oldest daughter was born.
All of a sudden, I had to be a mother, and my husband had to be a father.
And what did I know about being a mother?
Now, when you have a small baby, it's easy.
The baby has to be taken care of.
You learn how to take care of a baby.
You learn how to bathe a baby, how to feed it, how to love it.
That was the easy part.
When my daughter turned eight-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when my oldest daughter turned eight I almost had a nervous-- I almost had  a nervous breakdown.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because it-- [CRYING] then it hit me.
[CRYING] Sorry.
It hit me that [DRINKS] I was at a loss.
I didn't know how to be a mother to an eight-- to an eight-year-old, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's when my normal childhood stopped.
And I no longer had a role model now.
I never talked about this to anybody.
This was only going on in my own head, and I was-- it disturbed me, and it-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but I never had, felt the need to discuss it with anyone, because there was no opportunity.
Somehow, even though my husband was a psychologist-- he was not a clinical psychologist.
He was an experimental psychologist.
So-- but I don't know that I would have talked to him about it.
It-- this was a part of my life that was past and that somehow should not impinge on the present, as I saw it at that time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I always look at children, to this day, and I think, you know, this child is eight, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and what you do with an eight-year-old?
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
So anyway, we got over that period.
And the problem became less with my subsequent children.
I guess I learned how to be a mother.
Or I think I learned how to be a mother.
You'd have to ask my children [LAUGHS] how good a mother I was.

And, I guess, like all survivors I was a very, very protective mother, you know, terrified if they were a minute late and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very strict about the times they were allowed to go out and the times they had to be in, because I would go bananas they were-- you know, if I didn't know where they were.
Maybe it's normal.
I, I don't know.
But-- so it, it really hit me at that time that, uh, perhaps my upbringing, you know, wasn't quite what the normal course--
